query: vagus nerve innervation of head and neck muscles

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Laryngeal Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: Laryngeal innervation arises from the vagus nerve (cranial nerve X), which innervates the intrinsic laryngeal musculature. The extrinsic musculature innervation divides between the vagus nerve and the cervical rootlets.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Pharyngeal Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: Although primarily known as the motor nerve of the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles, the accessory nerve (11th cranial nerve or cranial nerve XI) also contributes some fibers to the vagus nerve and indirectly influences pharyngeal muscular function. Sympathetic and parasympathetic autonomic nerves also contribute to the pharyngeal plexus. These nerves regulate the vascular and glandular elements of the pharynx but do not contribute to pharyngeal muscular function.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Jugular Foramen -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Cranial nerve X plays a significant role in controlling the heart rate via parasympathetic innervation. Additionally, the nerve has inputs in the intestinal musculature to assist with peristalsis and digestion.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pharynx Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The pharyngeal plexus provides the sensory and motor innervation to the pharynx. This innervation forms via contributions from the vagus and glossopharyngeal nerves, as well as branches of the superior cervical ganglion. Specifically, the motor innervation of the STP muscle is mostly from the glossopharyngeal nerve, while the vagus nerve supplies the innervation to the other pharyngeal muscles. The pharyngeal muscles have a high nerve-muscle fiber ratio of 1:2 to 1:6 which allows for precisely coordinated pharyngeal functions. [2] The innervation of the cricopharyngeus muscle is from the pharyngeal plexus and the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx -- Muscles -- Extrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The inferior pharyngeal constrictor muscles extend from the cricoid and thyroid cartilages to the pharyngeal raphe. These muscles are innervated by the vagus nerve via branches of the pharyngeal plexus and recurrent laryngeal nerve and narrow the pharynx diameter to contribute to swallowing.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Stylopharyngeus Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: All the muscles of the pharynx receive innervation from the tenth cranial nerve (vagus nerve) except the stylopharyngeus, which is innervated by the ninth cranial nerve (glossopharyngeal nerve). From the nucleus ambiguous that lies in the floor of the fourth ventricle, motor  fibers travel in the glossopharyngeal nerve and innervate the muscle. After its exit from the cranial cavity, the glossopharyngeal nerve descends in the neck, running anterolaterally to the internal carotid artery. On reaching the inferior margin of the stylopharyngeus muscle, the nerve gives off several branches that provide motor innervations to this muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Muscles -- Extrinsic Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The extrinsic muscles of the larynx are found in bilateral pairs and contribute to the gross movement of the larynx. The innervation of these muscles varies, involving the ansa cervicalis, trigeminal nerve, facial nerve, glossopharyngeal nerve, and hypoglossal nerve. The pharyngeal constrictors and palatopharyngeus receive innervation from the glossopharyngeal, vagus, and spinal accessory nerves through the pharyngeal plexus.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Laryngeal Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The digastric muscle has 2 bellies with different innervations. The anterior belly is innervated by the mylohyoid nerve (a branch of the mandibular nerve). The posterior belly is innervated by the facial nerve (cranial nerve VII). Pharyngeal branches from the vagus nerve innervate the pharyngeal constrictors, which act with the suprahyoid muscles to elevate the larynx during swallowing. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Anterior Vagus Nerve -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: As the vagus nerve enters the abdominal cavity through the esophageal hiatus, it splits into an anterior trunk and a posterior trunk. The anterior trunk is mainly responsible for gastrointestinal parasympathetic innervation to the lesser curvature of the stomach, the pylorus, the biliary apparatus, and the gallbladder.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Laryngeal Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The laryngeal branches of the vagus nerve are the SLN and the RLN. The SLN has external and internal branches. The external branch primarily carries somatic motor fibers to the cricothyroid muscle. [11] The internal branch enters the larynx through a foramen in the thyrohyoid membrane, with the superior laryngeal artery providing somatosensory innervation to the mucosa of the glottis and supraglottis.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The laryngeal muscles receive innervation from two nerves, the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve and the recurrent laryngeal nerve. These nerves both arise from the vagus nerve (cranial nerve X) and provide both motor and sensory innervation to the intrinsic muscles of the larynx, excluding the interarytenoid muscles. The cricothyroid muscle is innervated solely by the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve while the recurrent laryngeal provides motor innervates to the other intrinsic laryngeal muscles. The internal branch of superior laryngeal nerve supplies sensory to the mucosa of the larynx. The nucleus ambiguous within the brain stem provides the motoneurons to the intrinsic laryngeal muscles while the hypoglossal nucleus provides the motoneurons to extrinsic muscles. [5] The recurrent laryngeal branch of the vagus nerve descends in the neck and on the left side loops around the aorta before ascending and entering the cricothyroid membrane to innervate the muscles. On the right the nerve loops around the subclavian and ascends to enter the larynx. Non-recurrent anatomic variants on the right do exist.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Anterior Vagus Nerve -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Palatoglossus Muscle (Glossopalatinus, Palatoglossal) -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The palatoglossus muscle receives innervation from the pharyngeal branch of the cranial nerve X (vagus nerve). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Anterior Vagus Nerve -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Several muscles receive innervation from the vagus nerve: the middle and inferior pharyngeal constrictor muscles, which are responsible for passage of food boluses; the palatoglossus, which elevates the posterior portion of the tongue on swallowing; and the laryngeal muscles, the aryepiglottic, thyroarytenoid, arytenoid, lateral and posterior cricoarytenoid, which are innervated by the recurrent laryngeal nerve. The entire length of the esophagus is also the recipient of motor innervation. [9] The vagus nerve innervates the crural area of the diaphragm (where the esophagus passes or esophageal hiatus). The tenth cranial nerve innervates the suspensory muscle of the duodenum or musculus suspensorius duodeni.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Skull -- Muscles -- Vagus Nerve (CN X)
retrieved_docs.content: The pharyngeal plexus is derived from CN X and innervates the musculus uvulae, the levator veli palatini, and many other muscles of the soft palate. Also innervated by the pharyngeal plexus are the salpingopharyngeus muscle and the superior, middle, and inferior pharyngeal constrictors.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Genioglossus Muscle -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The hypoglossal nerve also referred to as cranial nerve XII, is responsible for the innervation of both the intrinsic and extrinsic muscles; this includes the genioglossus muscles, as they are part of the extrinsic muscle group. Pleural receptors, when called upon (during an inhalation), send inhibitory afferents to the XII nucleus to make the genioglossus muscle more able to retract and better dilate the upper airway.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2794
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine is recognized as having a broader relationship with neurotransmitery, indicating that it functions as a neurotransmitter within the nervous system. This classification underscores acetylcholine’s role in transmitting signals between nerve cells and across neuromuscular junctions, which is fundamental to processes such as muscle contraction, autonomic nervous system regulation, and cognitive functions. Understanding acetylcholine’s position as a neurotransmitter is essential for comprehending its impact on both normal physiological signaling and various clinical conditions where its pathways may be altered.

retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1374
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and bethanechol are both classified as parasympathomimetics, a group of agents that mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. By stimulating cholinergic pathways, these drugs enhance parasympathetic tone, which can be therapeutically useful in conditions where increased muscarinic activity is desired. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action despite being used for different clinical indications.

In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.
